attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-week history of cellulitis over his right gluteal area. He has a medical history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). He has no medication allergies. His regular medications are metformin, gliclazide, salbutamol, and fluticasone/salmeterol. He smokes 20 cigarettes per day.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. Examination reveals a large area of cellulitis over the right gluteal region with patchy skin necrosis. The wound has not had any exposure with water. On palpation, there is crepitus present.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following intravenous antibiotic regimens is most appropriate for this man's presentation?
attributes.explanation:
The most appropriate intravenous antibiotic regimen for this patient with suspected necrotising fasciitis is clindamycin, meropenem and vancomycin. This combination provides broad-spectrum coverage against gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms, which are typically involved in necrotising fasciitis. Notably, this is the management for wounds without water exposure; such infections that are exposed to a water source are treated with the above three-drug regimen (clindamycin, meropenem, and vancomycin), as well as ciprofloxacin.

Clindamycin is particularly important in the treatment of necrotising fasciitis due to its ability to suppress toxin production by group A streptococci, a common causative organism. It also has excellent tissue penetration. Meropenem provides broad-spectrum coverage against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, including many anaerobes. Vancomycin adds coverage against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), which is an important consideration in severe soft tissue infections, especially in patients with risk factors such as poorly controlled diabetes.

Ceftriaxone and metronidazole would provide coverage against many gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms, but this combination lacks the toxin-suppressing effects of clindamycin, which is crucial in necrotising fasciitis. It also does not provide adequate coverage for MRSA.

Amoxicillin, metronidazole and gentamicin is a broad-spectrum combination, but amoxicillin may not be sufficient for the potentially resistant organisms in necrotising fasciitis, particularly in a patient with poorly controlled diabetes. This regimen also lacks specific coverage for MRSA.

Piperacillin/tazobactam and vancomycin provides broad-spectrum coverage including MRSA, but it lacks the specific toxin-suppressing effects of clindamycin, which is crucial in necrotising fasciitis.

Clindamycin and gentamicin alone would not provide sufficient gram-negative coverage for this potentially polymicrobial infection, and lacks specific coverage for MRSA.

It's important to note that while appropriate antibiotic therapy is crucial, the definitive treatment for necrotising fasciitis is urgent surgical debridement. The patient should be referred immediately for surgical evaluation alongside the initiation of antibiotic therapy. Additionally, supportive care, including fluid resuscitation and management of the patient's comorbidities (diabetes and COPD), is essential.
learningPoints: Necrotising fasciitis is a life-threatening soft tissue infection requiring prompt diagnosis and aggressive management.
The antibiotic regimen for necrotising fasciitis should cover gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms.
Clindamycin is particularly important in necrotising fasciitis due to its ability to suppress toxin production by group A streptococci.
references: eTG Necrotising skin and soft tissue infections. April 2019. https://tgldcdp.tg.org.au/viewTopic?etgAccess=true&guidelinePage=Antibiotic&topicfile=skin-soft-tissue-necrotising-infections&guidelinename=Antibiotic&sectionId=toc_d1e143#toc_d1e143

Hakkarainen TW, Kopari NM, Pham TN, Evans HL. Necrotizing soft tissue infections: review and current concepts in treatment, systems of care, and outcomes. Curr Probl Surg. 2014 Aug;51(8):344-62. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4199388/

Naidoo S, Zwane AM, Paruk A, Hardcastle TC. Diagnosis and Management of Severe Water-Related Skin and Soft Tissue Sepsis: A Summative Review of the Literature. Diagnostics (Basel). 2023 Jun 23;13(13):2150. doi: 10.3390/diagnostics13132150. PMID: 37443543; PMCID: PMC10340249. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10340249/

Guliyeva G, Huayllani MT, Sharma NT, Janis JE. Practical Review of Necrotizing Fasciitis: Principles and Evidence-based Management. Plast Reconstr Surg Glob Open. 2024 Jan 19;12(1):e5533. doi: 10.1097/GOX.0000000000005533. PMID: 38250213; PMCID: PMC10798703. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10798703/
